narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid LSAT score of 39 and undergraduate GPA of 3.10, both of which stand above the typical student's marks. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, estimated a 56.7% chance that you would not pass—tipping the scales just enough to result in the rejection of your application. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic decile rankings from Year 3 and Year 1 in school, which were both in the 2nd decile, well below the average of around the 5th or 6th decile for most students in the dataset. These lower rankings had the strongest pull toward the model's concern about bar exam failure, suggesting they signal potential challenges in sustaining high performance through law school. On a brighter note, your LSAT score pushed significantly in the opposite direction, toward a prediction of passing, as it exceeded the typical score of 36.7 and demonstrated your aptitude for the rigors ahead. Your undergraduate GPA, though slightly below the average of 3.21, also helped counterbalance the prediction by showing solid prior academic ability. Other factors, like your middle family income bracket—common among applicants—and your plan to attend full-time, like the vast majority (over 92%) of students, played smaller roles, with income slightly raising concerns and full-time status offering a minor boost. Compared to typical students who get approved, your decile rankings stand out as the biggest gap, while your LSAT gives you an edge many lack. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black woman trailblazer in law—it's the model's data-driven assessment of bar passage odds. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your school deciles, perhaps seeking targeted tutoring or academic coaching tailored to your background, and reapply once you've strengthened those areas. You're capable of turning this around, and many in similar situations have succeeded with focused effort. Keep pushing; your story is far from over.
